Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

AN GARDA SÍOCHÁNA
DMR North Central — Mountjoy Garda Station

VOLUME 12
CROSS-EXAMINATION, EVIDENCE
& PLAIN-CLOTHES OPERATIONS
The Garda Witness in the Box — Tactics, the Law of Evidence, and Non-Uniform
Duty

Companion volume to Garda Investigation Techniques — The Complete Operational Reference (Vols 1–11)

Page 1

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

Front Matter — About This Volume
This volume extends the eleven-volume Garda Investigation Techniques series into the part of
the job that decides whether good investigation survives contact with a courtroom: giving
evidence, withstanding cross-examination, and operating effectively in plain clothes. It is the
tactical companion to Volume 11 (files, statements and court craft) — where Volume 11 builds
the file and the cross-examination roadmaps, this volume drills the witness-box defence and the
underlying law of evidence. It is built for the reference moment — the night before a trial, the hour
before the book, the briefing before a covert tasking.

How this volume is organised
• Part A — Defence Cross-Examination Tactics & Counter-Responses. Every recognised
line of attack a defence advocate uses against a Garda witness, the trap inside it, the
disciplined counter, and the point of law behind it.
• Part B — The Law of Evidence for the Garda Witness. The Irish law of evidence, chapter
by chapter, filtered to what a member actually needs in the box: relevance and admissibility,
burden of proof, oral evidence and examination, unreliable evidence, hearsay, opinion and
expert evidence, improperly obtained evidence, confessions, and character.
• Part C — Plain-Clothes Operations. Non-uniform duty from tasking to testimony:
surveillance discipline, identification, covert and test-purchase awareness, officer safety,
and giving evidence as a plain-clothes member.
• Part D — Inference Interviews. The ss. 18, 19 and 19A inference procedure start to finish:
the provisions, the preconditions, the procedure, model wording for what to say, a worked
example, and an interview-plan template.
• Appendices & Indexes. A trap-to-law lookup, a detailed A–Z keyword search index, a case
index, and a statute & regulation index.

How to search this volume
Three routes in, depending on what you have in your hand:
• Know the heading? Use the Detailed Contents (next pages). Every Part, chapter and
lettered section is listed.
• Have a word, not a heading? Go to Appendix B — Keyword / Topic Search Index (A–Z).
Each term points to the section here and, where relevant, to the volume and chapter in Vols
1–11.
• Sitting in the trap right now? Go to Appendix A — Cross-Examination Trap → Law
Lookup. Find the question type, read the counter, jump to the law.
THE WORKING REFERENCE PRINCIPLE. This is a working reference, not legal advice. Irish
statute, case law and Garda operational policy continue to develop. Before relying on any
statement here for an operational or evidential decision, verify on revisedacts.lawreform.ie,
irishstatutebook.ie, vLex Ireland, BAILII or courts.ie. Where Garda HQ directives or operational
policy apply, those take precedence. Where doubt exists, supervisor consultation is the
operational standard.
SOURCING NOTE. Part A integrates the station Cross-Examination Tactics aide-memoire. Part
B mirrors the chapter and section structure of the standard Irish law-of-evidence treatment and
states settled Irish authority in summary form. Case names and citations are given so they can
be checked at source — confirm the current position before quoting any authority in court.

Page 2

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

Detailed Contents
Auto-generated from the headings below. In Word, right-click and choose Update Field to refresh
page numbers.

Page 3

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

Part A — Defence Cross-Examination Tactics &
Counter-Responses
A.1 Foundations — why you are being cross-examined, and the one
rule that defeats it
Cross-examination is not an attack on you personally. It is the mechanism by which the defence
tests the prosecution case. The right to cross-examine prosecution witnesses is an element of
the constitutional right to fair procedures and a fair trial, and it will be exercised in full. Your job is
not to win the exchange; it is to give accurate evidence and let it stand.
In re Haughey [1971] IR 217 — fair procedures include the right to cross-examine those who
give evidence against a person.
Donnelly v Ireland [1998] 1 IR 321 — the right to cross-examine is a component of the right
to a fair trial, though it is not unqualified (special-measures evidence, live link, etc.).
The objectives the advocate is pursuing
• To weaken the weight of your evidence (perception, memory, certainty).
• To attack your credibility or suggest bias or motive.
• To expose procedural or investigative gaps (caution, search, continuity, disclosure).
• To undermine identification.
• To obtain admissions through compound, loaded or rapid-fire questions.
THE GOLDEN RULE. Consistency between your notebook, your statement and your oral
evidence is your armour. Cross-examination has very little to work with when all three align.
Everything in Part A reduces to protecting that alignment — and the discipline starts at the
scene, in the notebook, long before the trial.
See also: Part B.3 (oral evidence, refreshing memory, previous inconsistent statements), B.4
(unreliable evidence, identification), B.7–B.8 (improperly obtained evidence and confessions).
Notebook and statement discipline is treated operationally in Vol 9 — Cross-Cutting Reference
(custody, disclosure, PULSE/PEMS).

A.2 Credibility attacks
The aim is to make you look biased, unreliable or out of your depth. The defeat is calm, specific,
and non-defensive.

The most answerable attacks if you prepared, the most damaging if you did not. Know your

A.5 Identification
Honest, confident witnesses are honestly and confidently wrong often enough that the law treats

A.7 Worked exchanges — the trap and the disciplined answer
Four short sequences showing the form of attack and the controlled reply. ‘Q’ is defence counsel;
‘A’ is you. The discipline is the same throughout: answer only what is asked, concede what is
true, hold what you know, and do not speculate.
1. Search power (the legislative-basis trap)
Q. Under what power did you search the car?
A. Section 23 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977, on reasonable suspicion that a controlled drug
was present.
Q. And the grounds for that suspicion?
A. The smell of cannabis from the vehicle and the movements I had observed, both noted at
the time.
Q. You can’t actually point to the section, can you?
A. I can. Section 23 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977.
Why it works. Know the power, the section and the grounds before you go in. Since B.7.1
(the JC test), whether any breach was deliberate and conscious is central — a precise,
prepared account protects admissibility. See A.4.
2. The caution (the confessions trap)
Q. Did you caution my client before he said anything?
A. Yes. At 21:14, before any questions, in the terms recorded in my notebook.
Q. You’re sure it wasn’t after he’d already spoken?
A. I am. The caution is timed in my notes at 21:14; his reply is timed at 21:16.
Why it works. Times and exact words win this exchange. The caution and the Judges’ Rules
sit behind it — B.8.3. Contemporaneous notes are the armour (A.2).
3. Identification (the dock-ID trap)
Q. You’re only saying it’s him because he’s the man in the dock.
A. No. I knew him before that night and recognised him at the time; I recorded his description
before any arrest.
Q. It was dark, though.
A. The forecourt was lit by overhead lighting and I had an unobstructed view from about fifteen
metres, as I noted at the time.
Why it works. Anchor identification in prior knowledge, a recorded first description and the
conditions from your notes — not the dock. This is the Casey danger, B.4.3. See A.5.
4. The compound question (the bundled-admission trap)
Q. You arrived, saw him strike the injured party, and he ran off — that’s right, isn’t it?
A. Let me take that in parts. I arrived and I saw him, yes. I did not see him strike anyone, and I
did not see him run.
Why it works. Break the bundle; agree only the true part. A.6.

Page 7

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

Part B — The Law of Evidence for the Garda Witness
This Part follows the chapter structure of the Irish law of evidence and pulls each chapter down
to what bears on a member giving evidence. Each section gives the principle in plain terms, the
leading Irish authority, and a short ‘in the box’ note. Authorities are named so they can be verified
at source.

B.0 How the law of evidence complements every volume
The law of evidence is not an offence category; it is the law of proof. It therefore complements
every volume in the series cross-cutting, the way Volume 9 does — not chapter-for-chapter, but
issue-for-issue. The same evidence chapters resurface in a property file, an assault file, a drugs
file and a sexual-offence file because each must prove its case by admissible evidence, survive a
voir dire, and withstand cross-examination.
So when this Part treats relevance, the burden of proof, oral evidence and cross-examination,
unreliable and identification evidence, hearsay, opinion, improperly obtained evidence,
confessions, character, privilege and documentary/real evidence, it is supplying the proof layer
beneath every offence volume. Appendix E maps each evidence topic to the volumes and
chapters it serves, so the complement is explicit rather than assumed.

B.1 Relevance and admissibility
Evidence is admissible only if it is relevant — if it makes a fact in issue more or less probable —
and is not excluded by a rule of law or by the trial judge’s discretion. Admissibility is a question of
law for the judge; weight is for the tribunal of fact.

B.1.1 Relevance and circumstantial evidence
Direct evidence proves a fact directly; circumstantial evidence proves facts from which a further
fact is inferred. A circumstantial case is built from strands that together exclude reasonable
alternatives.

B.1.2 The voir dire (‘trial within a trial’)
Disputed admissibility — typically of a confession or of evidence said to be unconstitutionally
obtained — is determined by the judge in the absence of the jury on a voir dire. As the member,
you may be examined and cross-examined twice: once on the voir dire on how the evidence was
obtained, and again before the jury on its content.

B.1.3 Discretion to exclude relevant evidence
Even relevant, technically admissible evidence may be excluded where its prejudicial effect
outweighs its probative value, or to ensure a fair trial.
IN THE BOX. Admissibility fights are won in the notebook. The cleaner your account of how
evidence was found, handled and recorded, the shorter the voir dire.

B.2 The burden and standard of proof
In a criminal trial the prosecution bears the legal burden of proving guilt beyond reasonable
doubt. The accused is presumed innocent. An evidential burden may shift to the accused to raise
a defence (e.g. self-defence), but the legal burden of disproving it generally remains with the
prosecution.
Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462 — the ‘golden thread’ — the prosecution must prove
guilt; the accused need not prove innocence.
People (DPP) v Forsey [2018] IESC 55 — Irish authority on reverse onus provisions and the
presumption of innocence — verify scope before relying.

Page 8

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

IN THE BOX. You prove facts, not guilt. Give the facts you observed accurately and completely;
the standard of proof is for the court, not for you to argue.

B.3 Oral evidence and examination
B.3.1 Competence and compellability
Most witnesses are both competent (legally permitted to give evidence) and compellable (can be
required to). Special rules apply to the accused, the spouse/civil partner of an accused, children,
and persons with a mental disability. Children and some others may give evidence otherwise
than on oath where they can give an intelligible account.

B.3.2 Examination-in-chief, leading questions and hostile witnesses
In chief, leading questions (questions suggesting the answer) are not permitted on matters in
dispute. Where a witness turns against the party calling them, the judge may declare the witness
hostile, allowing that party to cross-examine and to put a previous inconsistent statement.

B.3.3 Cross-examination — the rules behind Part A
Cross-examination may go to the issues and to credit. Counsel must put their case: matters they
intend to contradict must be put to the witness so the witness can answer them (the rule in
Browne v Dunn). Answers on purely collateral matters (going only to credit) are generally final
and cannot be contradicted, subject to exceptions (previous convictions, bias, reputation for
untruthfulness, physical/mental condition affecting reliability).
Browne v Dunn (1893) 6 R 67 (HL) — a party must put to a witness any matter on which it
intends to contradict that witness.

B.3.4 Refreshing memory — the notebook
A witness may refresh memory from a document made or verified by the witness
contemporaneously — at the time of the events or so soon afterwards that the facts were fresh.
This is the legal basis for using your notebook in the box. If you refresh from it, the document
may be inspected by the defence and you may be cross-examined on it; entries outside what you
refreshed from may be explored.
IN THE BOX. Make entries at the time or as soon as practicable, in sequence, without gaps,
alterations initialled. A notebook that is contemporaneous, complete and consistent with your
statement is the single most effective answer to most credibility attacks in Part A.

B.3.5 Previous consistent and inconsistent statements
As a rule a witness cannot bolster their own evidence with a prior consistent statement, subject
to exceptions (recent complaint, rebutting recent fabrication, statements forming part of the res
gestae, previous identification). A previous inconsistent statement can be put in crossexamination to undermine credibility; under s.16 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006 a witness’s
earlier statement may, in defined circumstances, be admitted as evidence of its truth where the
witness resiles from it.

B.3.6 Evidence by live television link and certificate evidence
Statute permits evidence by live link for certain witnesses (vulnerable witnesses, complainants in
sexual or violent cases, witnesses in fear or intimidated, witnesses outside the State). Certificate
evidence (e.g. of analysis, of certain procedural facts) is admissible subject to strict compliance
with the statutory conditions — a defect in the certificate can defeat it.
Cross-ref: complainant and vulnerable-witness procedure, Vol 6 — Sexual Offences;
intoxilyser/analysis certificates, Vol 7 — Road Traffic.

Page 9

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

B.4 Unreliable evidence — corroboration, accomplices and
identification
Some categories of evidence carry a recognised risk of being wrong or self-serving. The law
responds with corroboration requirements or warnings to the jury.

B.4.1 Corroboration and the accomplice warning
Corroboration is independent evidence implicating the accused in a material particular. A warning
about the danger of convicting on the uncorroborated evidence of an accomplice is long
established; modern statute has moved many warnings from mandatory to discretionary, but the
underlying caution remains.
The rationale is that an accomplice has an interest of his own to serve: he may minimise his own
involvement, shift blame, or fabricate to obtain favourable treatment. The traditional mandatory
warning was criticised as over-broad — a blanket rule applied even where the particular
accomplice had no such motive — and the modern approach treats the danger as a reason for
caution to be assessed case by case rather than as an automatic formula. The same logic now
extends beyond accomplices to any witness with a motive to fabricate (an informer given a
benefit, a co-accused, a witness in dispute with the accused): the existence of a motive is not
itself an automatic trigger for a formal warning, but it is a matter the tribunal must weigh.
Vetrovec v The Queen (1982, Can SC) — the influential critique of the rigid accomplice rule
— a flexible, fact-sensitive caution is preferable to a mandatory formula; cited in the Irish
treatment of the warning.

B.4.2 Witnesses who have obtained benefits; witness protection
Where a witness has received a benefit (e.g. immunity, sentence consideration, the Witness
Protection Programme), the jury should be alert to the motive that benefit may create. Disclosure
of such benefits is essential.

B.4.3 Identification evidence — the Casey warning
Visual identification is treated as a special danger because an honest, confident witness can be
honestly and confidently mistaken. The jury must be warned of this danger and of the need for
caution, especially where the identification is fleeting, in poor conditions, or by a stranger.
People (AG) v Casey (No 2) [1963] IR 33 — the standard warning on the dangers of visual
identification evidence — the ‘Casey warning’.
People (AG) v Casey (No 2) [1963] IR 33
Facts — A conviction rested substantially on visual identification by witnesses who were
honest and sure of themselves.
Held — Where a case depends wholly or substantially on visual identification, the jury must be
warned of the special need for caution — because honest, convincing witnesses have in the
past been shown to be mistaken, sometimes leading to wrongful convictions. The strength of a
witness’s confidence is not a measure of accuracy.
In the box — Your confidence is not the point — the anchor is. Record a first description
before identity is known, note prior knowledge of the person, and give the observation
conditions from your notes, not from the dock. This is the law behind A.3 and A.5.

CASE IN BRIEF

People (DPP) v O’Reilly [1990] 2 IR 415 — on identification-parade and pre-trial identification
procedures — verify current PULSE/identification procedure before relying.
IN THE BOX. Anchor any identification in something durable: prior knowledge of the person, a
formal identification procedure, distinctive features or actions — not the fact that the accused is
sitting in the dock. This is the law behind A.5.

Page 10

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

B.4.4 Lies, fabrication and false evidence by Garda witnesses
An accused’s lie can corroborate only if it is deliberate, relates to a material issue, is motivated
by guilt, and is shown to be a lie by independent evidence (the Lucas direction). The treatment
recognises, squarely, the category of false or mistaken evidence by Garda witnesses — the
strongest institutional reason for scrupulous notebook and statement discipline.

B.5 The rule against hearsay
An out-of-court statement is generally inadmissible to prove the truth of its contents — because
the maker cannot be cross-examined. A statement adduced for another purpose (that it was
made, its effect on the hearer, the state of mind it shows) is not hearsay; nor, generally, are
implied assertions and statements led only to show the fact of communication.

B.5.1 The principled (reliability) approach
Alongside the fixed common-law exceptions, the courts have recognised a more flexible,
principled approach under which an otherwise hearsay statement may be admitted where two
conditions are met: necessity (the maker is unavailable or cannot reasonably be produced) and
reliability (the circumstances in which the statement was made provide a sufficient guarantee of
its trustworthiness). This Canadian-influenced approach supplements, and does not abolish, the
established categories — confirm its current scope in Irish law before relying.

B.5.2 Principal exceptions a member meets
• Res gestae — statements so closely connected to an event that concoction is unlikely. The
recognised strands are: spontaneous statements made under the pressure of the event (the
distressed 999 call, the cry at the scene); statements accompanying and explaining a
relevant act; statements of contemporaneous physical sensation; and statements of
contemporaneous state of mind or emotion.
• Admissions and confessions — what the accused said against interest (governed by the
confession rules, B.8).
• Dying declarations and declarations against interest — narrow common-law exceptions.
• Statutory admission of statements — documentary/business records under the Criminal
Evidence Act 1992, Part II; and a witness’s previous statement admitted as evidence of its
truth under s.16 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006 where the witness resiles (B.3.5), each
subject to its statutory conditions.
IN THE BOX. Record the exact words a witness or suspect used and the circumstances (who
was present, how soon after the event, the speaker’s demeanour and apparent state). Whether a
statement is res gestae, falls within the principled approach, or is simply inadmissible hearsay
often turns on those recorded details.

B.6 Opinion and expert evidence
A witness ordinarily gives evidence of fact, not opinion. Two exceptions matter: non-expert
opinion on everyday matters (speed, identity, intoxication, the ordinary meaning of what was
seen), and expert opinion on matters beyond ordinary knowledge, given by a qualified,
independent expert whose duty is to the court.

B.6.1 Garda opinion and belief evidence by statute
Statute creates specific opinion/belief evidence roles, which must be operated exactly as the
section provides:
• s.3(2) Offences Against the State (Amendment) Act 1972 — a Chief Superintendent’s
belief that a person was a member of an unlawful organisation is admissible evidence of
membership.

Page 11

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

• Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 (and related provisions) — expert/analyst evidence on
identification and valuation of controlled drugs; see
• Garda opinion on the existence of a criminal organisation — statutory opinion evidence
introduced for organised-crime offences (verify the current section and conditions before
relying).
Cross-ref: drugs valuation and s.15A threshold, Vol 5 — Drugs; organised crime, Vol 10 —
Criminal Intelligence.

B.7 Improperly and unconstitutionally obtained evidence
Evidence may be challenged because of how it was obtained. Irish law distinguishes evidence
obtained in breach of a constitutional right from evidence merely obtained illegally (in breach of a
statute or common-law rule but not the Constitution).

B.7.1 The exclusionary rule — O’Brien → Kenny → JC
People (AG) v O’Brien [1965] IR 142 — founding distinction — evidence obtained by a
‘deliberate and conscious violation’ of constitutional rights is, absent extraordinary excusing
circumstances, inadmissible; merely illegally obtained evidence is subject to judicial discretion.
People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110 — hardened the rule — evidence obtained in breach
of a constitutional right is excluded regardless of the good faith of the member; the focus is on
the act, not the member’s state of mind.
People (DPP) v JC [2015] IESC 31
Facts — Gardaí investigating a series of robberies searched the suspect’s home on a warrant
issued under s.29 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 and obtained inculpatory
statements in custody. After the search, s.29 was declared unconstitutional in Damache, so the
entry had been unlawful. Applying Kenny, the trial judge excluded the statements and directed
an acquittal; the DPP appealed under s.23 of the Criminal Procedure Act 2010.
Held — By a 4:3 majority the Supreme Court overruled Kenny and reformulated the rule. The
onus is on the prosecution; where it shows the breach was not deliberate and conscious — e.g.
genuine inadvertence or reliance on a warrant only later found defective — the evidence may
be admitted. A deliberate, conscious, reckless or grossly negligent breach is still excluded, save
extraordinary excusing circumstances.
In the box — Be able to show that any breach was inadvertent, not deliberate — the prepared,
accurate account of how you acted is now directly relevant to admissibility. See B.7.3 and A.4.

CASE IN BRIEF

WHERE THE LAW HAS MOVED. For 25 years Kenny meant automatic exclusion regardless of
good faith. Since JC (2015) inadvertent breaches may be admitted. The test was applied again in
People (DPP) v Dwyer (Supreme Court, 2024): data retained in breach of EU law was treated as
obtained through a ‘subsequent legal development’ rather than a deliberate breach, and so was
not excluded. Treat any pre-2015 statement of ‘automatic exclusion’ as superseded.

B.7.2 Illegally (non-constitutionally) obtained evidence
Where the breach is of a statutory or common-law rule but not a constitutional right, admissibility
is at the trial judge’s discretion, balancing the public interest in convicting the guilty against the
public interest in protecting against unlawful conduct by the State.

B.7.3 Search and warrant validity
Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11
Facts — The accused’s home was searched on a warrant issued under s.29 of the Offences
Against the State Act 1939 by a Garda superintendent who was connected to the investigation.
Held — The Supreme Court held s.29 unconstitutional: a person who issues a search warrant
must be independent of the investigation. A member involved in the case cannot be the issuer.
In the box — Know who lawfully issues your warrant and that they are independent of the
investigation. A warrant defect is a standard cross-examination line — A.4.

CASE IN BRIEF

Page 12

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

WHERE THE LAW HAS MOVED. Following Damache, s.29 was replaced and several searchwarrant regimes were amended to require issue by a District Court judge or other independent
authority. Always confirm the current power and the correct issuing authority before relying on a
warrant.
IN THE BOX. Be able to state, without hesitation: the power, the section, the grounds, who
authorised it, and what was done. The JC test now turns on whether any breach was deliberate
and conscious — an honest, accurate, prepared account of how you acted is directly relevant to
admissibility.

B.8 Confessions
A confession is admissible only if voluntary and obtained fairly. Four overlapping controls
operate: constitutional rights, the voluntariness rule, the Judges’ Rules, and the custody and
electronic-recording regulations.

B.8.1 Constitutional rights — liberty and access to a solicitor
People (DPP) v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17
Facts — Gormley asked for a solicitor on arrest, but was interviewed before the solicitor
arrived and made admissions that were used to convict him. (In the joined case, White was
required to give forensic samples before legal advice.)
Held — Basic fairness applies from the moment of arrest. A suspect who has requested legal
advice must be given a reasonable opportunity to get it before questioning begins; questioning
should be deferred until then. Gormley’s admissions were excluded and his conviction quashed.
(White: there was no equivalent right to advice before giving compelled, objective forensic
samples.)
In the box — If a suspect has asked for a solicitor, do not begin or continue questioning until
they have had access to legal advice. This is the law behind A.4.

CASE IN BRIEF

People (DPP) v Doyle [2017] IESC 1
Facts — Doyle confessed to murder during his fifteenth interview. He argued his statements
should be excluded because he had no solicitor present in the interview room.
Held — By a 6:1 majority the Supreme Court held the constitutional right is a right of access to
legal advice — not a right to have a solicitor physically present during questioning. The
confession was not excluded on that ground.
In the box — Access before and between interviews is the constitutional minimum; presence
in the room is governed by current Garda policy, not by a constitutional right.

CASE IN BRIEF

WHERE THE LAW HAS MOVED. The position on solicitors has shifted in stages: Lavery v
Member in Charge, Carrickmacross [1999] 2 IR 390 (no right to a solicitor present) → Gormley
(2014: advice required before questioning) → Doyle (2017: still no constitutional right to
presence). Separately, since the DPP’s 2014 direction and the 2015 Code of Practice, Gardaí in
practice permit a requested solicitor to attend interviews. Operate to the current code, and verify
it before relying.

B.8.2 The voluntariness rule
People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] IR 1 — confessions must be voluntary and obtained in
compliance with basic fairness; a confession obtained by oppression or by threats or
inducements is inadmissible.
Oppression, threats, inducements, or conditions that sap the free will of the suspect render a
confession involuntary and inadmissible — a question decided on the voir dire (B.1.2).

B.8.3 The Judges’ Rules
The Judges’ Rules are administrative rules of practice for the questioning of suspects (when to
caution, recording answers, cautioning on charge). They are not statute; breach does not

Page 13

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

automatically exclude a statement, but it engages the judge’s discretion to exclude. Know the
caution wording and when it must be given.

B.8.4 Custody and electronic-recording regulations
• Criminal Justice Act 1984 (Treatment of Persons in Custody in Garda Síochána
Stations) Regulations 1987 (S.I. 119/1987) — govern detention, the member in charge,
notification of rights, access to a solicitor, rest, medical treatment and the custody record.
By s.7(3) of the 1984 Act a breach does not of itself render evidence inadmissible, but the
court may take it into account.
• Criminal Justice Act 1984 (Electronic Recording of Interviews) Regulations 1997 (S.I.
74/1997) — govern audio/video recording of interviews; non-compliance must be explained
and engages judicial discretion.

B.8.5 Corroboration, challenge and derived evidence
Under s.10 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1993 a jury must be advised of the danger of
convicting on a confession alone where there is no corroboration. Admissibility is challenged on
the voir dire; a confession can be edited (e.g. to remove references to a co-accused), and
evidence derived from an inadmissible confession may itself be challenged.
Cross-ref: detention, member-in-charge duties, the custody record and disclosure are treated
operationally in Vol 9 — Cross-Cutting Reference.

B.9 Character evidence
The accused’s bad character / previous convictions are generally inadmissible to suggest a
propensity to offend, subject to exceptions. The accused may put their good character in issue; if
they attack prosecution witnesses’ character or assert their own good character, they may lose
the ‘shield’ and become liable to cross-examination on their record. Similar-fact / misconduct
evidence is admissible only where its probative force is high enough to outweigh its prejudice.
IN THE BOX. Do not volunteer an accused’s previous convictions or other bad-character
material in your evidence. Disclosing the record without a proper basis can derail a trial. If in
doubt, answer the question asked and stop.

B.10 Privilege and public interest immunity
Some communications and material are protected from disclosure even though relevant. The
categories a member meets most often are legal professional privilege, the privilege against selfincrimination, without-prejudice communications, and public interest immunity (including informer
privilege).

B.10.1 Legal professional privilege
Legal professional privilege protects (a) confidential communications between a lawyer and client
made for the purpose of giving or receiving legal advice (legal advice privilege), and (b)
communications with third parties made for the dominant purpose of pending or contemplated
litigation (litigation privilege). For a claim to succeed the communication must be made within a
professional legal relationship and intended to be confidential. The relationship extends to inhouse and foreign lawyers. The privilege attaches to communications, not to facts that exist
independently of them; a pre-existing document does not become privileged merely by being
handed to a lawyer.

B.10.2 Public interest immunity and informer privilege
The State may resist disclosure of material whose release would harm the public interest — most
relevantly the identity of an informer or the methods of covert operations. This is balanced
against the accused’s right to a fair trial, and is resolved by the court, not in the witness box.
Where the disputed material may be necessary to establish innocence, the privilege yields.

Page 14

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

IN THE BOX. If asked about sources, methods, or another member’s confidential material, do
not disclose and do not improvise an answer — indicate that the matter may be privileged or
sensitive and that it is one for the court and your supervisors. See Part C.4 and Vol 10 (informant
handling).

B.11 Documentary and real evidence
Beyond oral testimony, two further classes of evidence carry their own rules of proof.

B.11.1 Documentary evidence
A document generally must be proved by production of the original and by evidence of its
authenticity and authorship, subject to statutory schemes that admit copies and business records
(the Criminal Evidence Act 1992, Part II, for documentary records compiled in the ordinary
course). Certificates and statutory forms (e.g. analysis certificates) are admissible on strict
compliance with their conditions (B.3.6).

B.11.2 Real evidence, exhibits and continuity
Real evidence — the recovered weapon, the seized drugs, the CCTV, the clothing — is proved
by production together with evidence connecting it to the case and accounting for its handling.
Continuity (chain of custody) is the evidential backbone: each person who handled the exhibit,
and what was done, from recovery to court. A gap in continuity is a standard line of attack (A.4).
IN THE BOX. Speak only to your own handling of an exhibit, refer to the exhibit book, and resist
guessing another member’s part of the chain. Continuity recorded contemporaneously is what
defeats the continuity challenge.
Cross-ref: exhibit management, forensic submission and FSI continuity in Vol 5 — Drugs and Vol
9 — Cross-Cutting Reference; this is the law behind A.4 and C.6.

Page 15

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

Part C — Plain-Clothes Operations
Plain-clothes (non-uniform) duty changes the evidential picture: you are harder to identify, your
authority is not visible, and your observations are more likely to be challenged on conditions and
continuity. This Part runs from tasking to testimony. It is operational guidance, not a grant of any
power — every action must rest on a lawful basis set out elsewhere in the series.

C.1 Purpose, authority and tasking
Plain-clothes duty serves observation, detection and engagement where a uniform presence
would defeat the purpose: covert observation of a location, foot surveillance, drugs and publicorder detection, and community work where approachability matters. Your powers are identical to
those on uniform duty — plain clothes is a tactic, not an extra authority.
• Operate to a clear tasking and briefing: objective, area, duration, command and control,
communications, and the lawful basis for any intended action.
• Record the authority for the operation and your own role within it.
• Have an identification and extraction plan before deployment — how you will identify
yourself as a member, and how you withdraw if compromised.
Cross-ref: the intelligence cycle, surveillance taskings and grading are in Vol 10 — Criminal
Intelligence; command and control and comms data in Vol 9.

C.2 Surveillance discipline and the observation log
Your evidence will live or die on the observation log. Keep it contemporaneous and specific. The
defence challenges in A.3 (conditions, duration, attention, distance) are aimed precisely at
unrecorded or vague observations.

C.2.1 What to record, as you go
• Times (start, key events, end) to the minute, and your position relative to what you
observed.
• Lighting, weather, line of sight, obstructions, and distance — estimated honestly.
• Description before identity: build the description (build, clothing, distinguishing features,
actions) before any name is attached.
• Who else was present and what each member could see from their position.
• Use of any optical aid (binoculars, camera, zoom) and its effect on what you could see.
IN THE BOX. When counsel says ‘it was dark and you were 40 metres away’, your answer is
your log: ‘The forecourt was lit by overhead lighting; I was approximately 25 metres away with an
unobstructed view, as recorded at the time.’ Specific, contemporaneous, and not adopted from
the questioner. See A.3.

C.3 Identification by plain-clothes members
Identification from covert observation attracts the full Casey danger (B.4.3). Strengthen it the
same way: a first description recorded before identity is known, prior knowledge of the person
where it exists, and a formal identification procedure where appropriate. Avoid building an
identification solely on a later dock sighting.

C.4 Test purchase, controlled and covert operations — awareness
Test-purchase, controlled-delivery and undercover work raise three recurring evidential issues.
Treat each as a planning item, not an afterthought:

Page 16

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

• The entrapment line. Evidence of an offence the accused was ready and willing to commit
is generally admissible; actively inciting an otherwise unwilling person to offend invites
challenge and possible exclusion. Operate to providing opportunity, not creating intent.
• Authorisation and records. Covert and surveillance activity must be properly authorised
and logged under the applicable statutory and policy framework; the authorisation is itself
disclosable material.
• Disclosure and informant protection. Balance the duty of disclosure against the
protection of methods and sources; this is a supervisory and, where necessary, legal
decision — do not resolve it alone in the box.
Cross-ref: HUMINT/OSINT/surveillance sourcing and handling, Vol 10 — Criminal Intelligence;
drugs powers and s.15A, Vol 5 — Drugs.

C.5 Unmarked vehicles, officer safety and identifying as a member
• Have a rehearsed, unambiguous means of identifying yourself as a Garda at the point of
intervention — the moment of greatest risk of being mistaken for a threat, by the public or by
uniform colleagues.
• Maintain officer-safety discipline appropriate to working without the visible deterrent of a
uniform; brief contingencies and extraction.
• Record the use and role of any unmarked vehicle where it bears on the evidence
(observation platform, pursuit, stop).

C.6 Giving evidence as a plain-clothes member
Bring the two earlier Parts together. The plain-clothes member in the box must establish three
things the uniform member often takes for granted:
• Your role and authority. Explain, plainly, that you were on duty in plain clothes under a
specific tasking, and that your powers were those of any member.
• The observation conditions. Give them from your log — position, distance, lighting, line of
sight, duration — and defend each element separately (A.3).
• Continuity. Speak to your own handling of any exhibit and to the records; refer to the
exhibit book; do not guess another member’s handling (A.4).
THE THREAD. Tasking → contemporaneous log → clean continuity → consistent statement →
calm, specific oral evidence. The same alignment that defeats cross-examination in Part A is
built, for the plain-clothes member, at C.2. Consistency is the armour.

Page 17

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

Part D — Inference Interviews (ss. 18, 19, 19A CJA
1984)
This Part is a start-to-finish guide to conducting an inference interview: the statutory provisions
that allow a court to draw an adverse inference from a suspect’s failure or refusal to account, the
preconditions that must be satisfied for the inference to be available, the procedure from
preparation to file, and model wording for what to say. The provisions are powerful but technical
— an inference is only available if every precondition is met, so the value is entirely in getting the
steps and the wording right.
VERIFY THE WORDING. The model explanations in D.6 capture the statutory effect in ordinary
language, but Garda training, PEMS and HQ directions set the approved form of words and the
recording procedure. Use the current approved wording; the models here are a guide to content,
not a substitute for the official script.

D.1 What an inference interview is — and what it is not
The ordinary position protects the right to silence: if a suspect stays silent, the jury is told nothing
about it, and silence is not evidence of guilt. The inference provisions are a limited, statutory
exception. In defined circumstances, and only if strict preconditions are met, a court may draw
‘such inferences as appear proper’ from a suspect’s failure or refusal to account for something,
and may treat that failure as capable of amounting to corroboration of other evidence. The
inference never stands alone — a person cannot be convicted solely or mainly on it.
An inference interview is therefore not a way to compel an answer or to punish silence. It is a
procedure that puts a specific matter to the suspect, explains in ordinary language the
consequence of not accounting for it, gives a real opportunity to answer (including legal advice
first), and records what happens — so that, if the matter is left unexplained, the court may later
attach weight to that.

D.2 The three provisions at a glance
All three apply to arrestable offences (offences carrying five years’ imprisonment or more) and
are the Irish equivalents of ss. 36, 37 and 34 of the UK CJPOA 1994.
Section

Triggers an inference from…

Extra element you must satisfy

s. 18

Failure/refusal to account for an
object, substance or mark (or the
condition of clothing/footwear) on
the person, in their possession, or in
the place where they were arrested.

You must reasonably believe the
object/mark may be attributable to the
suspect’s participation in the offence,
and you must tell the suspect of that
belief and request an account.

s. 19

Failure/refusal to account for the
suspect’s presence at a particular
place at or about the time the
offence was committed.

You must reasonably believe their
presence may be attributable to
participation in the offence, and you
must tell the suspect of that belief and
request an account.

s. 19A

Failure to mention a fact later relied
on in defence, being a fact which in
the circumstances clearly called for
an explanation at the time of
questioning, charge, or notice of
prosecution.

The broadest provision. No ‘reasonable
belief’ step — but the fact must be one
that clearly called for an explanation,
and the court has regard to when the
fact was first mentioned.

Related provisions extend inferences to specific contexts: s. 2 of the Offences Against the
State (Amendment) Act 1998 (failure to answer questions material to the investigation of certain

Page 18

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

offences), and the application of ss. 18–19A to detentions under s. 2 of the Criminal Justice
(Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 and s. 50 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007. These carry their own
conditions — verify the specific section before relying.

D.3 The constitutional and ECHR backdrop
The right to silence is constitutionally protected but not absolute; it may be restricted by
proportionate statutory measures. The inference regime survives because of its safeguards —
legal advice, ordinary-language warning, recording, and the bar on conviction on an inference
alone.
Heaney v Ireland [1996] 1 IR 580 — the right to silence is not absolute and may be restricted
by proportionate measures serving a pressing social need.
Rock v Ireland [1997] 3 IR 484 — the Supreme Court upheld the inference provisions (then
ss. 18–19) as a proportionate restriction on the right to silence, given the safeguards attaching
to them.
Murray (John) v United Kingdom (1996) 22 EHRR 29 (ECtHR)
Facts — A suspect was questioned and inferences were later drawn from his silence under the
Northern Ireland regime; he had been denied access to a solicitor at the start of detention.
Held — Drawing inferences from silence is not in itself a breach of the right to a fair trial,
provided proper safeguards exist and the inference is not the sole or main basis of conviction.
But access to a solicitor at the early stage of police questioning is of paramount importance
where inferences may be drawn — denying it can render the trial unfair.
In the box — This is why legal advice before the failure is a hard precondition (D.4). No
solicitor opportunity → no inference. It connects directly to Gormley (B.8.1).

CASE IN BRIEF

D.4 The preconditions — all must be satisfied
If any one of these is missing, the inference is not available and putting it may taint the interview.
Treat this as a checklist before and during the interview:
• Arrestable offence. The offence under investigation carries five years’ imprisonment or
more.
• Lawful detention. The suspect is lawfully detained and the custody regime (B.8.4, Vol 9) is
being complied with.
• Solicitor opportunity first. The suspect was afforded a reasonable opportunity to consult a
solicitor before the failure or refusal occurred. In practice: do not seek to invoke an inference
until legal advice has been offered and, if requested, obtained.
• Reasonable belief (s. 18 / s. 19). For objects/marks or presence, you genuinely and
reasonably believe the matter may be attributable to participation in the offence — and you
tell the suspect so.
• Ordinary-language explanation. The suspect is told, in ordinary language, what the effect
of failing or refusing to account might be (D.6).
• A clear request and a real opportunity. You put the specific object/mark/presence/fact
and ask the suspect to account for it, giving a genuine opportunity to do so.
• Electronic recording. The questioning is recorded by electronic or similar means (or the
suspect consents in writing to it not being recorded). Without this, s. 19A does not apply,
and recording is the practical norm for all three.
• Corroboration only. The inference can support other evidence but cannot be the sole or
main basis of conviction, and there must be a case to answer on the other evidence.

D.5 Start to finish — the procedure
D.5.1 Before the interview
• Confirm the offence is arrestable and the detention is lawful and properly authorised.

Page 19

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

• Identify which section(s) apply, and to exactly what: list each object, mark, substance, place,
or fact you intend to put.
• For s. 18 / s. 19, form and be able to articulate your reasonable belief: why this object/mark
or presence may be attributable to participation in this offence.
• Ensure the solicitor opportunity has been given — offered, and if requested, facilitated —
and that this is recorded. Hold the inference questions until then.
• Confirm the interview will be electronically recorded; check the equipment.
• Brief the interview team: who leads, who notes, the order matters are put, the exhibits to
hand.

D.5.2 Build the interview plan
Plan the inference element as a distinct, deliberate phase — not something dropped in mid-flow.
Use the template at D.9. For each matter, plan: the exact item to put, the basis of your belief, the
ordinary-language explanation, and how you will record the response.

D.5.3 In the interview — the sequence
For each object/mark/presence/fact, work through the same disciplined sequence, on the
recording:
• 1. Caution. Ensure the ordinary caution has been administered and the suspect
understands it.
• 2. Confirm legal advice. State on the recording that the suspect was offered, and had the
opportunity to obtain, legal advice.
• 3. Put the matter. Describe the specific object/mark/place/fact precisely. For s. 18/19, state
your belief that it may be connected to their participation in the offence.
• 4. Explain the effect (ordinary language). Give the explanation at D.6 in plain terms and
check the suspect understands it.
• 5. Request an account. Ask the suspect to account for it, and give a genuine opportunity to
answer.
• 6. Record the response. Note exactly what the suspect says or does — an account, a
refusal, ‘no comment’, or silence — in their own words and behaviour.
DISCIPLINE. One matter at a time. Do not bundle several objects into one compound question
(the same fault the defence exploits in A.6). Put each clearly, explain, request, record — then
move to the next.

D.5.4 Closing and after
• Close the recording in the normal way; preserve and label the recording as an exhibit
(continuity, B.11.2).
• In your statement and cover report, set out: the section relied on, the matter put, the belief
stated (s. 18/19), the explanation given, the opportunity for legal advice, and the suspect’s
response — with times.
• In the DPP file, flag the inference clearly so it can be presented properly at trial. Coverreport structure: Vol 11 (court craft) and the Mountjoy cover-report template.

D.6 What to say — model ordinary-language explanations
MODEL ONLY. Use the current Garda-approved wording. The following captures the statutory
content in plain terms and can be adapted to the matter being put.
s. 18 — objects, substances or marks
“I am investigating [offence]. [Describe the object / substance / mark, and where it was found.]
I believe this may be connected to your taking part in that offence.
I am asking you to account for it. I must explain in ordinary language: if you fail or refuse to
account for it, a court may later treat that failure or refusal as supporting other evidence

Page 20

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

against you. You do not have to say anything, and you cannot be convicted on this failure
alone, but a court may take it into account.
You have had the opportunity to speak to a solicitor. Do you wish to account for [the
object/mark]?”
s. 19 — presence at a particular place
“You were [at / near] [place] at or about the time [offence] was committed. I believe your
presence there may be connected to your taking part in that offence.
I am asking you to account for why you were there. If you fail or refuse to account for it, a court
may later treat that failure as supporting other evidence against you. You do not have to say
anything, you cannot be convicted on this alone, but a court may take it into account. You have
had the opportunity to consult a solicitor. Can you account for your presence at [place]?”
s. 19A — a fact later relied on in defence
“There is something here that, in these circumstances, clearly calls for an explanation from
you: [state the matter]. If you later rely in your defence on a fact that you could tell us now but
choose not to, a court may draw such inferences as it thinks proper from your failure to
mention it now, and may treat that as supporting other evidence against you.
You do not have to say anything, and you cannot be convicted on this alone, but a court may
take into account anything you fail to mention now and later rely on. You have had the
opportunity to consult a solicitor. Is there anything you wish to say about [the matter]?”
Note the tension a suspect may feel between the ordinary caution (‘you do not have to say
anything’) and the inference explanation (‘but a court may hold your silence against you’). Give
both clearly and check understanding; confusion has been raised as a ground of challenge, so
the clarity of your explanation matters.

D.7 Worked example — s. 18 (object on the suspect)
Suspect arrested for a burglary; a screwdriver matching tool-marks at the scene is found in his
jacket. After the caution and a solicitor consultation:
Member. “I’m investigating the burglary at [address] this morning. This screwdriver was in
your jacket pocket. I believe it may be connected to your involvement in that burglary. I’m
asking you to account for it. If you fail or refuse to account for it, a court may treat that as
supporting other evidence against you. You don’t have to say anything and you can’t be
convicted on this alone, but a court may take it into account. You’ve had the chance to speak
to your solicitor. Can you account for the screwdriver?”
Suspect. “No comment.”
Record. The matter put, the belief stated, the explanation given, the solicitor opportunity, and
the ‘no comment’ response are all on the recording and noted with times — so the failure to
account is available to the court as corroboration of the tool-mark and recovery evidence.

D.8 Pitfalls that defeat an inference
Each of these is a precondition failure and a cross-examination target — see Part A:
• No solicitor opportunity before the failure — the single most common defeat (Murray;
Gormley). Offer and facilitate legal advice first.
• No, or unclear, ordinary-language explanation — if the suspect was not properly told the
effect, the inference falls.
• Belief not held or not stated (s. 18/19) — you must actually hold a reasonable belief and
tell the suspect of it.
• Matter not put clearly / bundled — vague or compound questions undermine the ‘failure
to account’. Put each matter precisely (A.6).
• Not recorded — no electronic recording (and no written consent to its absence) means s.
19A does not apply.

Page 21

Garda Investigation Techniques — Vol 12: Cross-Examination, Evidence & Plain-Clothes

• Treating the inference as the case — there must be other evidence; the inference only
corroborates.
IN THE BOX. Expect to be cross-examined on every precondition: the timing of legal advice, the
words you used, the basis of your belief, and the recording. As everywhere in this volume, the
contemporaneous record and a precise, prepared account are the armour (A.2, A.4).

D.9 Inference interview plan — template
Complete one block per matter to be put. Keep it with the interview plan and the cover report.

yrs+)

s. 18 / s. 19 / s. 19A

place / fact)

(s. 18/19)

(time / outcome)

surviving cross-examination on procedure A.4.

Appendix E — Evidence Complement Map (by volume
& chapter)
The law of evidence in Part B is cross-cutting: it supplies the proof layer beneath every offence
volume. This map shows, for each volume, the evidence topics that most often arise and where
they are treated in Part B. It is the concrete answer to “does the evidence material complement
every chapter?” — it complements them by issue (proof, admissibility, cross-examination), not by
offence label.
Volume

Evidence topics that most complement it

Part B §

Vol 1 — Property
Crime

Relevance & circumstantial proof; recent-possession
inference; identification; CCTV & documentary; real
evidence & continuity; search/warrant validity.

B.1, B.4.3, B.5, B.7,
B.11

Vol 2 — Crimes
Against Person

Identification (Casey); res gestae (999 call, scene cries);
medical/injury expert; self-defence evidential burden;
confessions.

B.2, B.4.3, B.5, B.6,
B.8

Vol 3 — Public Order

Garda observation as oral evidence; lay opinion
(intoxication, behaviour); identification; CCTV/real
evidence.

B.3, B.4.3, B.6, B.11

Vol 4 — Domestic
Violence

Hostile/recanting witness & s.16 CJA 2006; res gestae 999
call; special measures / live link; coercive-control pattern
evidence.

B.3.5, B.3.6, B.5, B.9

Vol 5 — Drugs

Expert/analyst & valuation evidence; statutory
presumptions & burden (s.15A); search & warrant validity;
continuity/FSI; confessions.

B.2, B.6, B.7, B.8,
B.11.2

Vol 6 — Sexual
Offences

Complainant corroboration/warning; recent complaint &
previous consistent statements; special measures / live
link; character & sexual-history rules.

B.3.5, B.3.6, B.4, B.9

Vol 7 — Road Traffic

Certificate & statutory-form evidence; lay/expert opinion
(impairment); specimen continuity; strict procedural
compliance.

B.3.6, B.6, B.11.1,
B.11.2

Vol 8 — Special
Incidents

Expert/medical & mental-health opinion; documentary &
hearsay (missing persons, sudden death); public interest
material.

B.5, B.6, B.10.2,
B.11.1

Vol 9 — CrossCutting

Improperly/unconstitutionally obtained evidence;
confessions & custody; disclosure & public interest
immunity; the voir dire; continuity. (Closest sibling to Part
B.)

B.1.2, B.7, B.8, B.10,
B.11.2

Vol 10 — Criminal
Intelligence

Garda opinion/belief evidence; informer privilege & PII;
surveillance product, disclosure & hearsay in intelligence.

B.6.1, B.10.2, B.5

Vol 11 — Files,
Statements & Court
Craft

All of Part B underpins cover reports and crossexamination roadmaps; especially oral evidence & crossexamination, unreliable/identification, confessions. Volume
12 is its tactical twin.

B.3, B.4, B.7, B.8 (→
Parts A & C)

HOW TO READ IT. Pick the volume you are working in, read across to the evidence topics that
typically decide the file, and jump to the Part B section. Every offence volume draws on several
evidence topics — which is why the evidence material is best held once, here, and crossreferenced, rather than repeated in each volume.
